metadata.title: NIST Artificial Intelligence Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0) — Complete Subcategory Catalog
metadata.remarks: Community OSCAL catalog representing all 72 subcategories from the NIST AI RMF 1.0 (NIST AI 100-1, January 2023). Source: https://airc.nist.gov/airmf-resources/airmf/5-sec-core/. Controls are marked behavioral=true where fixture-based testing can produce direct evidence, and behavioral=false for organizational/procedural subcategories.

groups.title: GOVERN — Cultivating and implementing organizational practices that promote AI risk management

controls.title: GOVERN 1.1 — Legal and regulatory requirements

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Legal and regulatory requirements involving AI are understood, managed, and documented.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Procedural control: organizational documentation required. Behavioral evidence: fixture cases tagged with applicable regulatory frameworks verify the AI system meets behavioral requirements of those regulations.

controls.title: GOVERN 1.2 — Trustworthy AI characteristics integrated into policies

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The characteristics of trustworthy AI are integrated into organizational policies, processes, procedures, and practices.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: fixture suite coverage across trustworthiness dimensions (safety, security, explainability, privacy, fairness, accountability).

controls.title: GOVERN 1.3 — Risk management level based on risk tolerance

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Processes, procedures, and practices are in place to determine the needed level of risk management activities based on the organization's risk tolerance.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: risk_tier field in fixture metadata calibrates evaluation intensity; high-risk fixtures have higher coverage and effectiveness thresholds.

controls.title: GOVERN 1.4 — Transparent risk management process

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The risk management process and its outcomes are established through transparent policies, procedures, and other controls based on organizational risk priorities.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: signed evidence tarballs with full evaluation traces provide transparent audit record of risk management outcomes.

controls.title: GOVERN 1.5 — Ongoing monitoring and periodic review

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Ongoing monitoring and periodic review of the risk management process and its outcomes are planned and organizational roles and responsibilities clearly defined, including determining the frequency of periodic review.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: behavioral drift index computed across time-windowed evaluation runs; run history demonstrating cadence.

controls.title: GOVERN 1.6 — AI system inventory

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Mechanisms are in place to inventory AI systems and are resourced according to organizational risk priorities.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Procedural control. Behavioral evidence: fixture suite catalog with skill_or_agent metadata provides machine-readable AI system inventory.

controls.title: GOVERN 1.7 — Decommissioning and phase-out processes

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Processes and procedures are in place for decommissioning and phasing out AI systems safely and in a manner that does not increase risks or decrease the organization's trustworthiness.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: behavioral coverage completeness — documented test suite provides a re-attestation baseline enabling safe decommission decisions.

controls.title: GOVERN 2.1 — Documented roles and responsibilities for AI risk

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Roles and responsibilities and lines of communication related to mapping, measuring, and managing AI risks are documented and are clear to individuals and teams throughout the organization.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Procedural control. Behavioral evidence: certification_track field in fixture metadata reflects ownership and approval chain.

controls.title: GOVERN 2.2 — AI risk management training

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The organization's personnel and partners receive AI risk management training to enable them to perform their duties and responsibilities consistent with related policies, procedures, and agreements.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Procedural control. Behavioral evidence: rubric dimensions testing whether AI system itself can explain its risk management approach.

controls.title: GOVERN 2.3 — Executive responsibility for AI risk decisions

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Executive leadership of the organization takes responsibility for decisions about risks associated with AI system development and deployment.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Procedural control: organizational accountability documentation required.

controls.title: GOVERN 3.1 — Diverse team for AI risk decisions

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Decision-making related to mapping, measuring, and managing AI risks throughout the lifecycle is informed by a diverse team (e.g., diversity of demographics, disciplines, experience, expertise, and backgrounds).

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: fixture suite covers diverse user demographics, languages, and cultural contexts in test cases.

controls.title: GOVERN 3.2 — Roles for human-AI configurations and oversight

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Policies and procedures are in place to define and differentiate roles and responsibilities for human-AI configurations and oversight of AI systems.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: escalation precision/recall metrics; fixture cases verifying human-in-the-loop handoff works correctly.

controls.title: GOVERN 4.1 — Safety-first mindset in AI development

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Organizational policies and practices are in place to foster a critical thinking and safety-first mindset in the design, development, deployment, and uses of AI systems to minimize potential negative impacts.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: adversarial and safety-tagged fixture cases demonstrate safety-first testing posture; injection resistance.

controls.title: GOVERN 4.2 — Documented risks and impacts

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Organizational teams document the risks and potential impacts of the AI technology they design, develop, deploy, evaluate, and use, and they communicate about the impacts more broadly.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: signed evidence tarballs with metrics and findings constitute the documented risk record.

controls.title: GOVERN 4.3 — AI testing, incident identification, and information sharing

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Organizational practices are in place to enable AI testing, identification of incidents, and information sharing.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: structured fixture-based evaluation with run IDs, exportable evidence tarballs, and bundle registry for information sharing.

controls.title: GOVERN 5.1 — External feedback integration

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Organizational policies and practices are in place to collect, consider, prioritize, and integrate feedback from those external to the team that developed or deployed the AI system regarding the potential individual and societal impacts related to AI risks.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: fixture cases generated from external stakeholder feedback; new test cases reflecting reported failures.

controls.title: GOVERN 5.2 — Mechanisms to incorporate adjudicated feedback

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Mechanisms are established to enable the team that developed or deployed AI systems to regularly incorporate adjudicated feedback from relevant AI actors into system design and implementation.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: audit suggest pipeline (agc audit suggest) for incorporating failure-mode feedback into system prompt improvements.

controls.title: GOVERN 6.1 — Third-party AI risk policies

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Policies and procedures are in place that address AI risks associated with third-party entities, including risks of infringement of a third-party's intellectual property or other rights.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: PHI/PII leakage rate in model outputs; data_handling field in fixture metadata documenting data provenance.

controls.title: GOVERN 6.2 — Contingency processes for third-party AI failures

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Contingency processes are in place to handle failures or incidents in third-party data or AI systems deemed to be high-risk.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: error-handling test coverage; fixture cases verifying graceful failure when dependencies are unavailable.

groups.title: MAP — Categorizing AI risks in context

controls.title: MAP 1.1 — Intended purposes, beneficial uses, and context documented

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Intended purposes, potentially beneficial uses, context-specific laws, norms and expectations, and prospective settings in which the AI system will be deployed are understood and documented.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: skill_or_agent field and fixture descriptions document intended use; happy-path cases verify intended purpose is met.

controls.title: MAP 1.2 — Interdisciplinary AI actors and diversity

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Interdisciplinary AI actors, competencies, skills, and capacities for establishing context reflect demographic diversity and broad domain and user experience expertise, and their participation is documented.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: fixture cases authored from diverse domain perspectives; evaluation rubrics include domain-expert-defined criteria.

controls.title: MAP 1.3 — Organizational mission and AI goals documented

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The organization's mission and relevant goals for AI technology are understood and documented.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Procedural control: mission documentation required. Behavioral evidence: fixture suite goals align with organizational mission.

controls.title: MAP 1.4 — Business value and context defined

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The business value or context of business use has been clearly defined or, in the case of assessing existing AI systems, re-evaluated.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: fixture case descriptions document the business scenario and value proposition each case tests.

controls.title: MAP 1.5 — Organizational risk tolerances documented

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Organizational risk tolerances are determined and documented.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: risk_tier field in fixture metadata; effectiveness_threshold configuration reflecting organizational risk tolerance.

controls.title: MAP 1.6 — System requirements with socio-technical implications

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: System requirements (e.g., 'the system shall respect the privacy of its users') are elicited from and understood by relevant AI actors. Design decisions take socio-technical implications into account to address AI risks.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: privacy requirements expressed as fixture assertions; PHI leakage rate verifying privacy requirements are met.

controls.title: MAP 2.1 — AI system tasks and methods defined

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The specific tasks and methods used to implement the tasks that the AI system will support are defined (e.g., classifiers, generative models, recommenders).

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: fixture cases map directly to specific task types; generator_model field documents the model type used.

controls.title: MAP 2.2 — AI system knowledge limits documented

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Information about the AI system's knowledge limits and how system output may be utilized and overseen by humans is documented. Documentation provides sufficient information to assist relevant AI actors when making decisions and taking subsequent actions.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: fixture cases testing out-of-scope queries; negative cases verifying refusals; AI output qualification language tested via rubric.

controls.title: MAP 2.3 — Scientific integrity and TEVV considerations

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Scientific integrity and TEVV (Test, Evaluate, Verify, Validate) considerations are identified and documented, including those related to experimental design, data collection and selection, system trustworthiness, and construct validation.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: confidence calibration score (ECE); discrimination score measuring test quality; rubric construct validity.

controls.title: MAP 3.1 — Potential benefits examined

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Potential benefits of intended AI system functionality and performance are examined and documented.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: happy-path fixture cases document and verify the intended beneficial use cases.

controls.title: MAP 3.2 — Potential costs and non-monetary impacts examined

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Potential costs, including non-monetary costs, which result from expected or realized AI errors or system functionality and trustworthiness, are examined and documented.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: failure case analysis via agc audit; error-handling and negative case coverage.

controls.title: MAP 3.3 — Targeted application scope specified

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Targeted application scope is specified and documented based on the system's capability, established context, and AI system categorization.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: fixture metadata (skill_or_agent, risk_tier, data_handling) precisely scopes the application context.

controls.title: MAP 3.4 — Operator proficiency processes defined

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Processes for operator and practitioner proficiency with AI system performance and trustworthiness, and relevant technical standards and certifications, are defined, assessed, and documented.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: certification_track field in fixture metadata documents proficiency and certification status.

controls.title: MAP 3.5 — Human oversight processes defined

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Processes for human oversight are defined, assessed, and documented in accordance with organizational policies from the govern function.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: escalation latency (p50/p95 ms), escalation precision/recall; multi-turn fixture cases verifying human oversight handoff.

controls.title: MAP 4.1 — Third-party data and AI technology legal risks

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Approaches for mapping AI technology and legal risks of its components, including the use of third-party data or software, are in place, followed, and documented.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: data_handling field (synthetic-only, no-pii, pii-reviewed) documents data provenance and third-party risk.

controls.title: MAP 4.2 — Internal risk controls for AI system components

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Internal risk controls for components of the AI system, including third-party AI technologies, are identified and documented.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: bundle manifest with SHA256 hashes documents component integrity; trust-check verifies registry attestation.

controls.title: MAP 5.1 — Likelihood and magnitude of identified impacts

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Likelihood and magnitude of each identified impact (both potentially beneficial and harmful) based on expected use, past uses of AI systems in similar contexts, public incident reports, feedback from those external to the team, or other data are identified and documented.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: confidence calibration (ECE) measures whether model confidence accurately reflects actual impact likelihood.

controls.title: MAP 5.2 — Practices for feedback on positive, negative, and unanticipated impacts

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Practices and personnel for supporting regular engagement with relevant AI actors and integrating feedback about positive, negative, and unanticipated impacts are in place and documented.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: agc audit pipeline captures failure modes; fixture suite updated from feedback to cover unanticipated impact scenarios.

groups.title: MEASURE — Analysing and assessing AI risks

controls.title: MEASURE 1.1 — Measurement approaches and metrics for AI risk

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Approaches and metrics for measurement of AI risks enumerated during the map function are selected for implementation starting with the most significant AI risks. The risks or trustworthiness characteristics that will not or cannot be measured are properly documented.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: behavioral coverage completeness score across 7-category risk taxonomy; documented rationale for included and excluded test categories.

controls.title: MEASURE 1.2 — Appropriateness and effectiveness of metrics regularly assessed

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Appropriateness of AI metrics and effectiveness of existing controls are regularly assessed and updated, including reports of errors and potential impacts on affected communities.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: drift index computed across runs detects metric effectiveness degradation; calibration score tracks metric reliability over time.

controls.title: MEASURE 1.3 — Independent assessment involvement

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Internal experts who did not serve as front-line developers for the system and/or independent assessors are involved in regular assessments and updates. Domain experts and affected communities are consulted as necessary.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: judge-evaluated rubric scores from an independent LLM judge (not the generator model); domain expert rubric authorship.

controls.title: MEASURE 2.1 — Test sets, metrics, and TEVV tools documented

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Test sets, metrics, and details about the tools used during TEVV are documented.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: fixture YAML files document test sets; run JSON documents metrics and tool versions; evidence tarballs preserve full TEVV record.

controls.title: MEASURE 2.2 — Human subjects and population representativeness in evaluations

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Evaluations involving human subjects meet applicable requirements and are representative of the relevant population.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: data_handling field documents whether PII from real subjects is used; fixture cases cover representative population demographics.

controls.title: MEASURE 2.3 — AI system performance demonstrated for deployment-similar conditions

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: AI system performance or assurance criteria are measured qualitatively or quantitatively and demonstrated for conditions similar to deployment setting(s). Measures are documented.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: effectiveness score, pass rate, and latency metrics across fixture cases that mirror production scenarios; live mode evaluation results.

controls.title: MEASURE 2.4 — AI system functionality monitored in production

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The functionality and behavior of the AI system and its components are monitored when in production.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: behavioral drift index across post-deployment evaluation runs; cadence of evaluation runs in run history.

controls.title: MEASURE 2.5 — AI system validity and reliability demonstrated

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The AI system to be deployed is demonstrated to be valid and reliable. Limitations of the generalizability beyond the conditions under which the technology was developed are documented.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: confidence calibration (ECE); flakiness rate; discrimination score per case measuring test value and reliability.

controls.title: MEASURE 2.6 — AI system evaluated for safety risks

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The AI system is evaluated regularly for safety risks. The AI system to be deployed is demonstrated to be safe, its residual negative risk does not exceed the risk tolerance, and it can fail safely. Safety metrics reflect system reliability and robustness, real-time monitoring, and response times for AI system failures.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: adversarial test pass rate; injection resistance score; error-handling coverage; fail-safe behavior in negative fixture cases.

controls.title: MEASURE 2.7 — AI system security and resilience evaluated

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: AI system security and resilience are evaluated and documented.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: prompt injection resistance score across adversarial fixture cases; security-tagged case coverage.

controls.title: MEASURE 2.8 — Transparency and accountability risks examined

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Risks associated with transparency and accountability, as identified in the map function, are examined and documented.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: hallucination density metric; rubric dimensions for explanation quality and citation accuracy.

controls.title: MEASURE 2.9 — AI model explained, validated, and documented

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The AI model is explained, validated, and documented, and AI system output is interpreted within its context to inform responsible use and governance.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: judge rationale field in eval scores; rubric dimensions for explanation quality; confidence calibration.

controls.title: MEASURE 2.10 — Privacy risk examined

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Privacy risk of the AI system is examined and documented.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: PHI/PII leakage rate; data_handling field; privacy-tagged fixture cases.

controls.title: MEASURE 2.11 — Fairness and bias evaluated

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Fairness and bias, as identified in the map function, are evaluated and results are documented.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: fixture cases covering diverse demographic inputs; rubric dimensions for fairness and non-discrimination; bias-detection test coverage.

controls.title: MEASURE 2.12 — Environmental impact and sustainability assessed

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Environmental impact and sustainability of AI model training and management activities are assessed and documented.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: token counts and estimated_cost_usd fields in run summary provide proxy for computational resource consumption.

controls.title: MEASURE 2.13 — TEVV metrics and processes effectiveness evaluated

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Effectiveness of the employed TEVV metrics and processes in the measure function are evaluated and documented.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: discrimination score per case (measures whether a test is actually discriminating); calibration score (measures whether scores predict outcomes).

controls.title: MEASURE 3.1 — Existing, unanticipated, and emergent AI risks tracked

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Approaches, personnel, and documentation are in place to regularly identify and track existing, unanticipated, and emergent AI risks based on factors such as intended and actual performance in deployed contexts.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: behavioral drift detection; agc audit root-cause classification of failure modes; run history tracking over time.

controls.title: MEASURE 3.2 — Risk tracking for difficult-to-assess settings

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Risk tracking approaches are considered for settings where AI risks are difficult to assess using currently available measurement techniques or where metrics are not yet available.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: agc compliance gaps command identifies controls with no measurement coverage; documented rationale for unmeasurable risks.

controls.title: MEASURE 3.3 — Feedback processes for end users to report problems

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Feedback processes for end users and impacted communities to report problems and appeal system outcomes are established and integrated into AI system evaluation metrics.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: fixture cases generated from reported real-world failures; appeal scenarios in test suite.

controls.title: MEASURE 4.1 — Measurement approaches connected to deployment context

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Measurement approaches for identifying AI risks are connected to deployment context(s) and informed through consultation with domain experts and other end users. Approaches are documented.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: domain-specific fixture tags (healthcare, financial, legal) connecting metrics to deployment context; domain expert rubric authorship.

controls.title: MEASURE 4.2 — Measurement results validated against deployment context

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Measurement results regarding AI system trustworthiness in deployment context(s) are informed by input from domain experts and relevant AI actors to validate whether the system is performing consistently as intended. Results are documented.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: judge-evaluated rubric scores from domain-expert-authored rubrics; OSCAL Assessment Results for auditor review.

controls.title: MEASURE 4.3 — Performance improvements and declines from field data

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Measurable performance improvements or declines based on consultations with relevant AI actors, including affected communities, and field data about context-relevant risks and trustworthiness characteristics are identified and documented.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: behavioral drift index comparing evaluation runs across time periods; model version comparison via agc carousel.

groups.title: MANAGE — Prioritizing and addressing AI risks

controls.title: MANAGE 1.1 — Determination to proceed with AI system development/deployment

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: A determination is made as to whether the AI system achieves its intended purposes and stated objectives and whether its development or deployment should proceed.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: pass rate and effectiveness score against defined thresholds provide objective proceed/no-proceed signal; certification_track field tracks promotion decisions.

controls.title: MANAGE 1.2 — AI risk treatment prioritized by impact and likelihood

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Treatment of documented AI risks is prioritized based on impact, likelihood, and available resources or methods.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: risk_tier and priority labels in fixture metadata; discrimination score identifying highest-value test cases.

controls.title: MANAGE 1.3 — Responses to high-priority AI risks developed

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Responses to the AI risks deemed high priority are developed, planned, and documented. Risk response options can include mitigating, transferring, avoiding, or accepting.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: agc audit suggested_fixes document specific risk response actions; injection resistance demonstrates implemented mitigation.

controls.title: MANAGE 1.4 — Residual risks to downstream acquirers documented

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Negative residual risks (defined as the sum of all unmitigated risks) to both downstream acquirers of AI systems and end users are documented.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: Certification Scope and Limitations section in evidence export report documents residual risks and re-attestation requirements.

controls.title: MANAGE 2.1 — Resources for AI risk management and non-AI alternatives considered

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Resources required to manage AI risks are taken into account, along with viable non-AI alternative systems, approaches, or methods, to reduce the magnitude or likelihood of potential impacts.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: cost metrics (gen_cost_usd, judge_cost_usd) in run summary document resource consumption; fixture cases verify when AI should defer to non-AI approaches.

controls.title: MANAGE 2.2 — Mechanisms to sustain value of deployed AI systems

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Mechanisms are in place and applied to sustain the value of deployed AI systems.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: re-attestation workflow (90-day grace period on model deprecation); regular evaluation runs maintaining current certification.

controls.title: MANAGE 2.3 — Response to previously unknown risks

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Procedures are followed to respond to and recover from a previously unknown risk when it is identified.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: agc audit identifies novel failure modes; fixture cases added in response to discovered risks.

controls.title: MANAGE 2.4 — Mechanisms to supersede or deactivate AI systems with poor performance

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Mechanisms are in place and applied, and responsibilities are assigned and understood, to supersede, disengage, or deactivate AI systems that demonstrate performance or outcomes inconsistent with intended use.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: certification track demotion workflow; behavioral drift index triggering re-evaluation; regression gate in agc compare.

controls.title: MANAGE 3.1 — Third-party AI risk monitoring

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: AI risks and benefits from third-party resources are regularly monitored, and risk controls are applied and documented.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: bundle trust-check command verifying third-party bundle attestation; drift index detecting third-party model changes.

controls.title: MANAGE 3.2 — Pre-trained models monitored as part of regular maintenance

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Pre-trained models which are used for development are monitored as part of AI system regular monitoring and maintenance.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: generator_model field in run summary; model-scoped attestation in compliance reports; behavioral drift index per model version.

controls.title: MANAGE 4.1 — Post-deployment monitoring plans implemented

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Post-deployment AI system monitoring plans are implemented, including mechanisms for capturing and evaluating input from users and other relevant AI actors, appeal and override, decommissioning, incident response, recovery, and change management.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: behavioral drift index across post-deployment runs; run history maintained in SQLite DB; OSCAL Assessment Results documenting ongoing monitoring.

controls.title: MANAGE 4.2 — Continual improvement activities integrated into AI system updates

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Measurable activities for continual improvements are integrated into AI system updates and include regular engagement with interested parties, including relevant AI actors.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: agc optimize pipeline and agc audit suggest providing measurable improvement loop; version-tagged fixture bundles tracking improvement over time.

controls.title: MANAGE 4.3 — Incidents and errors communicated to relevant AI actors

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Incidents and errors are communicated to relevant AI actors, including affected communities. Processes for tracking, responding to, and recovering from incidents and errors are followed and documented.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: structured failure records in run JSON; audit findings with root-cause classification; registry-published evidence for incident transparency.
